thinking.text:
The script was created but I realize this approach still has problems: when a new frame shows a newly executed command, it's in the transcript, but my comparison logic might not work correctly because frames can have varying numbers of commands. Also, as the game progresses, earlier commands might scroll off screen, causing them to appear as \"new\" again when they reappear.

Let me think about this differently. The video shows the game being played. The transcript shows commands that have been executed. The last line with '>' shows the command currently being typed. 

A better approach: 
1. Look at each frame, get all '>' lines
2. The LAST '>' line is the current typing command
3. The SECOND TO LAST '>' line (if exists) is the most recently executed command
4. Track when the second-to-last command changes - that means a new command was just executed

Actually, even simpler: I can look at the last '>' line of each frame. When that line changes completely (not just more characters typed), a new command has been started. When I see a new command being typed, the previous one was completed.

Or even better: I should extract the text of each frame, find the LAST line starting with '>', and that's the command currently being typed. When the command changes from one frame to the next (e.g., from 'n' to 'get'), the previous command was executed.

Let me try a simpler approach: just look at the last '>' line from each frame and track changes.
